title: CPU & GPU — The Computer's Engines
key_insight: The CPU is one super-smart worker. The GPU is thousands of simple workers. AI needs the thousands.
body:
## CPU: One Smart Worker

Imagine the world's fastest chef. They can cook any dish perfectly, but only one thing at a time.

That's a CPU (Central Processing Unit). It:
- Executes one instruction at a time
- Is incredibly fast (billions of instructions per second)
- Can handle any type of task
- Has 4–32 'workers' (cores) in a modern laptop

## GPU: An Army of Simple Workers

Now imagine 10,000 simple chefs who can each only make toast. Useless for cooking a feast — unless your feast is 10,000 pieces of toast.

That's a GPU (Graphics Processing Unit). It:
- Has thousands of simple cores (NVIDIA H100: 16,896 cores)
- Each core is much weaker than a CPU core
- But they all work at the same time (in parallel)
- Originally designed for graphics — turning 3D models into pixels simultaneously

## Why GPUs for AI?

Training a neural network means doing the same math on thousands of numbers simultaneously:

```
For each of 256 training examples:
  For each of 4096 neurons:
    Compute: output = weight × input + bias
```

These computations are completely independent of each other. The GPU does all 256 × 4096 = ~1 million computations at the same time.

A CPU does them one (or 16, with 16 cores) at a time.

## Analogy Summary

| | CPU | GPU |
|---|---|---|
| Like | 1 brilliant chef | 10,000 toast-makers |
| Best for | Complex sequential tasks | Simple parallel tasks |
| AI training | Too slow | Perfect |
| Running your browser | Perfect | Overkill |
common_mistakes: Thinking a faster CPU makes AI training faster — GPU speed matters far more for training
Confusing CPU cores (4–32) with GPU cores (thousands) — totally different scale
Assuming you need a GPU to learn AI — you can learn everything on CPU, it just trains slower
mini_exercise: Open Python and try: import time; start=time.time(); total=sum(range(10_000_000)); print(time.time()-start). This runs on CPU. For comparison, GPU PyTorch operations run 10-100× faster for matrix math.
